title: Container Creation, Process Lifecycle, CLI State, and Cleanup · Release Workshop
summary: A platform pairing session must produce the first working deterministic container lifecycle runbook for container creation, process lifecycle, cli state, and cleanup, with predictions recorded before each simulated build or lifecycle change. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of deterministic container lifecycle runbook while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish create, run, start, attach, exec, stop, kill, restart, pause, wait, remove, and auto-remove state transitions.
Apply names, environment, working directory, user, entrypoint, command, labels, hosts, and platform overrides with correct precedence.
Choose foreground, detached, interactive, TTY, attach, and log behavior while preserving standard streams and exit evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# docker-basics cumulative container delivery portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never connect to a Docker daemon, mount a socket, or execute learner commands.
workspace: docker
version-baseline:
  engine: 29.6.1
  compose: 5.3.1
  buildx: 0.35.0
  buildkit: 0.31.1
  dockerfile-frontend: 1.25
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-environment
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Docker Create Run Start Boundary
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop operator inherits 3 similarly named containers: one created, one running, one exited with code 137, and one carrying the only writable-layer report. Inspect identity and state, preserve evidence, then perform label-scoped cleanup. Competency probe: Distinguish create, run, start, attach, exec, stop, kill, restart, pause, wait, remove, and auto-remove state transitions.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Docker Create Run Start Boundary.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish create, run, start, attach, exec, stop, kill, restart, pause, wait, remove, and auto-remove state transitions. steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels
steps.instruction:
Append the Docker evidence item headed "  # Evidence: docker-cleanup-scope-labels-z11ndh". Tailor its container-lifecycle.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run Docker commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Docker Cleanup Scope Labels into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels, keep Docker Cleanup Scope Labels connected to mechanically reviewable Docker artifact and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: docker-cleanup-scope-labels-z11ndh
  - competency: docker-cleanup-scope-labels
    artifact: container-lifecycle.yaml
    decision: cleanup scope labels
    scenario-variant: 1
    environment: ephemeral development host with constrained disk and memory
    fault-injection: invalidate one cache input without changing application source
    retained-proof: timestamped lifecycle transition and bounded cleanup result
    case-key: z11ndh
    change: |
      create: immutable-configured-container
      start: same-container-identity
      inspect: state-before-mutation
      cleanup: label-scoped-and-previewed
    verify: docker container inspect training-web --format json
    failure: Reject name-only cleanup, unbounded prune, or a claim that stop and remove preserve writable-layer state.
    repair: Use explicit IDs or labels, inspect before mutation, capture exit state, and remove only the scoped disposable resource.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use labels, explicit identifiers, dry evidence, and dependency checks to remove only intended containers and resources.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels, name the daemon authority, immutable identity, process, storage, network, secret, and cleanup boundary, Docker Cleanup Scope Labels precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels, trace this concrete input and predict build, image, container, mount, network, Compose, policy, and teardown evidence before reviewing it: A workshop operator inherits 2 similarly named containers: one created, one running, one exited with code 137, and one carrying the only writable-layer report. Inspect identity and state, preserve evidence, then perform label-scoped cleanup. Competency probe: Use labels, explicit identifiers, dry evidence, and dependency checks to remove only intended containers and resources.

For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Cleanup Scope Labels, challenge the documented misconception with manifest, inspect, lifecycle, failure, repair, and transfer evidence: docker system prune is the safest first response to any disk or naming problem.
